title: JAMB Cut-Off Marks Explained: What They Mean and How They Affect Your Admission
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have your JAMB score. You know the number out of 400. You have heard about cut-off marks, but you are not entirely sure what they mean or how they affect your chances of admission. The term cut-off mark is used in multiple ways, and the confusion can lead to false hope or unnecessary despair. Here is what the different cut-off marks actually mean and how they interact to determine whether you get into your chosen course.

The Three Types of Cut-Off Marks

There is not one cut-off mark. There are three, and they operate at different levels. The first is the general JAMB cut-off mark, which is set annually by JAMB in consultation with universities, polytechnics, and colleges of education. This is the minimum score required to be eligible for admission into any tertiary institution in Nigeria. For universities, the general cut-off has typically been between 140 and 200 in recent years. For polytechnics and colleges of education, it is lower, often around 100 to 120. If your score is below the general cut-off for your institution type, you are not eligible for admission that year. Period.

The second is the institutional cut-off mark, which is set by each individual institution. A university may decide that its minimum acceptable score is higher than the general JAMB cut-off. The University of Lagos or the University of Ibadan, for example, might set an institutional cut-off of 200. This means that no candidate scoring below 200 will be considered for admission to that university, regardless of the course they applied for.

The third is the departmental cut-off mark, which is set by individual departments within a university. This is the most important number for your specific application. A university might have an institutional cut-off of 200, but the Department of Medicine might have a departmental cut-off of 280. The Department of Sociology might have a departmental cut-off of 210. Your score must meet the departmental cut-off for your specific course, not just the general or institutional cut-off. The departmental cut-off is determined by supply and demand. Courses with many applicants and few spaces have high cut-offs. Courses with fewer applicants have lower cut-offs.

How Cut-Off Marks Are Determined

Departmental cut-off marks are not fixed in advance. They emerge from the application process. A department has a certain number of available spaces, perhaps fifty or one hundred. Thousands of candidates may apply. The department ranks all applicants by a combination of their JAMB score and their post-UTME screening score. It then offers admission to the top candidates until the spaces are filled. The score of the last admitted candidate becomes the effective departmental cut-off for that year.

This means that cut-off marks can vary from year to year. A course that had a cut-off of 240 last year might have a cut-off of 260 this year if the applicant pool is stronger, or 220 if it is weaker. Historical cut-off marks give you a sense of what is competitive, but they are not guarantees. The only certainty is that a higher JAMB score improves your position in the ranking and increases your chances of falling above the eventual cut-off.

The Role of Post-UTME in Determining Admission

Your JAMB score is not the only number that matters. Most universities conduct a post-UTME screening exercise. This is an additional exam or assessment administered by the university itself. Your final ranking for admission is typically a weighted combination of your JAMB score and your post-UTME score. The weighting varies by institution. A common formula is fifty percent JAMB and fifty percent post-UTME. Some universities weight JAMB more heavily. Others weight post-UTME more heavily.

This means that a candidate with a moderate JAMB score can still gain admission with an excellent post-UTME performance. Conversely, a candidate with a high JAMB score cannot afford to neglect the post-UTME. The post-UTME is your opportunity to improve your ranking and secure admission even if your JAMB score is slightly below the historical departmental cut-off for your intended course. Prepare for it as seriously as you prepared for JAMB.

What to Do If Your Score Is Below the Cut-Off

If your JAMB score is below the general or institutional cut-off, you have several options. You can apply to a different institution with a lower cut-off. You can change your course to one with a lower departmental cut-off within the same institution. You can apply to a polytechnic or college of education, which typically have lower cut-offs. You can retake JAMB the following year and aim for a higher score.

The JAMB portal allows you to change your course or institution after results are released and before admissions begin. This is called a change of course or change of institution. It is a valuable option if your original choices are out of reach. Be strategic about the change. Research the typical cut-off marks for the course and institution you are switching to. Do not switch to another course that is equally competitive and face the same problem again.

The Bottom Line

JAMB cut-off marks operate at three levels: the general JAMB cut-off for all institutions, the institutional cut-off for a specific university, and the departmental cut-off for your specific course. Your JAMB score must meet all three to secure admission. Departmental cut-offs are determined by competition and vary from year to year. Your post-UTME score combines with your JAMB score to determine your final ranking. If your score is below the required cut-off, consider changing your course, changing your institution, or exploring polytechnic and college of education pathways. The cut-off is not a wall. It is a threshold, and there are paths around it if you are strategic.
